text.title: Design Token Hierarchy and Naming
text.summary: Three-level token hierarchy (primitive → semantic → component), naming conventions, and version control strategy.

rule.statement: Structure tokens in three levels: primitive (raw values), semantic (purpose aliases), component (per-component overrides). Semantic tokens reference primitives; component tokens reference semantics. Never skip a level or create circular references.
rule.rationale: The hierarchy decouples value from purpose. Changing a brand colour requires updating one primitive; all semantic aliases that reference it update automatically, propagating to every component token.

example.note: Adding dark mode: only semantic tokens change; component tokens automatically inherit the new values.

antipattern.description: Using value-based names for semantic tokens: color-blue-500 as a semantic token name.
antipattern.reason: The name encodes the value; when the brand colour changes to green, all usages of "blue-500" are semantically wrong even if they render correctly.

rule.statement: Primitive token names describe what they are (color-blue-500, spacing-4). Semantic token names describe purpose (color-text-primary, spacing-content-gap). Component token names describe the component and property (button-padding-horizontal).
rule.rationale: Purpose-based names survive redesigns; value-based names break the abstraction as soon as the underlying value changes.

rule.statement: Store all token files in Git alongside application code. Apply semantic versioning: breaking value changes = major bump; new tokens = minor; fixes = patch.
rule.rationale: Tokens in design tools only (Figma, Sketch) are inaccessible to CI/CD and drift from implementation. Version control makes token changes reviewable, diffable, and rollback-safe.

text.reference: Primitive token file with full color, spacing, font, radius, shadow scales
Semantic light theme tokens referencing primitives
